page: Vultr vhp-2c-2gb-intel is an entry-level virtual server belonging to the High Performance family. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an Intel Xeon Cascadelake processor with 2 shared vCPUs, consisting of 2 physical cores and 1 thread per core, running at a clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 2.0 GB of system memory, providing a memory-to-core ratio of 1.0 GB per core, and is bundled with 60 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. No GPU resources are included in this profile.

Benchmark evaluations place this server in the lower tiers of performance. The stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU tests yield scores of 1384.38 and 2740.38 respectively, both falling into the bottom 25% of tested hardware. Memory bandwidth is a notable bottleneck, with large-block read and write speeds of 13.19 GB/sec and 11.09 GB/sec, placing it in the bottom 10%. Database operations and static web serving throughput also rank in the bottom 10%, with Redis SET operations achieving 2.81 million operations per second.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this server offers a budget-friendly entry point for lightweight workloads due to its shared CPU allocation and low resource density. However, the primary tradeoff is the constrained memory capacity of 2.0 GB and the shared CPU model, which limits its utility for resource-intensive applications. It is best suited for basic general-purpose tasks, lightweight background processes, simple static web serving, or development and testing environments that do not require sustained high CPU or memory throughput.
description: Vultr vhp-2c-2gb-intel is an entry-level virtual server in the High Performance family, utilizing an x86_64 Intel Xeon Cascadelake processor with 2 shared vCPUs and 2.0 GB of RAM. The system is equipped with 60 GB of local NVMe SSD storage and lacks a dedicated GPU. Performance benchmarks indicate that this configuration sits in the lower tiers, with stress-ng CPU scores and memory bandwidth tests consistently ranking in the bottom 10% to 25% of evaluated hardware. Due to its shared CPU allocation and limited memory-to-core ratio of 1.0 GB per core, this server presents a budget-friendly option for low-demand workloads. It is best suited for lightweight tasks such as basic static web serving, development environments, and simple background processes rather than resource-intensive database or computational workloads.
